0-study.html.title: 0 Study
0-study.html.description:
https://www.coursera.org/learn/learning-how-to-learnLearn through metaphor and analogy.Focused mode and diffuse mode, you can't be in both modes at the same time. In diffuse mode you can explore and learn new things, that with focus you simply can't: the green pattern on the left is not easily reached.In diffuse mode we let our brain wander. During exercise we leverage diffuse mode.Another analogy could be a flashlight: diffuse: casts light in a broad area, not very strongly on anything in particular focus: casts light more brightly in a narrow area diffuse: broad ranging way of thinking (things we're familiar with) focus: help keep your thoughts concentrated (exploring new things) Why you feel bad when about to study.When looking at something we don't relish doing, neural discomfort happens, this is our brain trying to stop that stimulation by diverting attention to something more pleasant. Good news is that not long after this discomfort disappears.It allows to start, it does not matter if you push through without taking the break if you're midway through something set a limit for the day: today I'm going for 10 pomodoros promote focus Teacher explains that usually scientific terms are abstract and are difficult for our brain to comprehend because we cannot point to something we can see of feel. She makes the example of a cow, explaining how easy it is to understand what the word "cow" means.It's important to practice ideas and concepts present in science to help enhance and strengthen the neural connections.Working memory: RAM. Long term memory: SDD.Spaced repetition is the answer.Sleep is how the brain keeps itself clean and healthy. Ideas are tidied up. Strengthen areas we might need or want.Plant the seeds for diffuse mode by engaging in focus mode: go over what you're learning right before a nap or before going to sleep, you've got increased chances of dreaming about it. A step further is setting in mind the will to dream about that material. Dreaming about what you're studying can substantially enhance the ability to understand.Unite bits of information together through mearning.Chunks are pieces of information. Bound together through meaning or use. Conceptual chunks are mental leaps that unite scattered bits of information through meaning. Focusing your attention to connect parts of the brain to tie together ideas is an important part of the focused mode of learning. It is also often what helps get you started in creating a chunk
Picture an octopus, the octopus of attention that slips its tentacles through those slots of working memory when necessary to help you make connections to information that you might have in various parts of your brain.Learn little by little then piece together everything.As an example in learning math: focus on "why is this the next thing I should do", rather then "why does this step work?". focus undivided attentions to what you're learning - the Chunking-Octopus can't reach very well into the slots of working memory
understanding the basic idea of what you're trying to chunk practice Context means learning when to use (and when not to use) the technique instead of some other. An example is when you skim through a chapter to get a big picture understanding of what you're about to read.
This is a top down approach. While chunking is bottom up. Both are critical to understanding.Chunking means learning how to use the technique instead of some other.This is how we learnAfter you've read, look away and see what you remember of what you've just read.Do the recall in different locations so that you're not bound to the visual cues of the environment where learning happens.Flashcards are the best way to do retrieval practice.Looking at a solution is not enough to grasping it. Keeping the book opened in front of us when repeating gives the illusion we got the concepts, it gives a false sense of knowing what the subject is about.It allows to make repairs in your thinking flaws. Mistakes help correct your thinking.Learning how to learn We need to test ourself on the material [...] test is a form of identifying the errors. [...] it's not about listening and regurgitating
Free course taught by Dr. Terry Sejnowski https://www.coursera.org/learn/learning-how-to-learn/Dr. Terry Sejnowski How to Improve at Learning Using Neuroscience & AIOptimal Protocols for Studying & LearningProcrastinationBeat it by recognizing cues and not indulging.Practice makes permanentAnalogy + metaphorTest yourselfNever cramStrengthen connections between neurons (synapses) Weaken connections between neurons (synapses) Creating connections between existing neurons (synapses)Neuro plasticityOffset forgettingGo over the material right before sleep to strengthen learning.Have at least 8 hours sleepPomodoro Try with 25/5, in those resting minute do something fun, go on with the DM screen project for examplePhysical activityBring back your mind to what you're doing, it's meant to be difficult. That strain cues the neuro circuits that it needs to change, that neuro plasticity needs to happen. It's good.Meditation to improve focus#NSDR (Non-Sleep Deep Rest) with Dr. Andrew Huberman - YouTubefocus (be alert), nervous system is like "hey this is important"Cold shower in the morning, hot shower in the evening; the former being one of the most effective changes I've ever implemented.When explaining make notes, write, trying to come up with quick visuals that will help you remember.Self testingIn class Take notes on the remarkable, jot down ideas and things to rememberAt home try to remember what the professor said in class expand that in the .md note double check with registration and book references

info_showCustomIcons.description: Show custom icons for files and folders

info_showDefaultFolderIcons.description: Show a default icon of a folder for every folder in the tree

info_showDefaultFileIcons.description: Show a default icon of a file for every file in the tree

info_defaultFolderIcon.description: The icon to use for folders. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_defaultFileIcon.description: The icon to use for files. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_defaultMediaIcon.description: The icon to use for media files. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_exposeStartingPath.description: Whether or not to show the current file in the file tree when the page is first loaded

info_displayTitle.description: Descriptive title to show above the feature

info_showOrphanNodes.description: Show nodes that are not connected to any other nodes.

info_showAttachments.description: Show attachments like images and PDFs as nodes in the graph.

info_allowGlobalGraph.description: Allow the user to view the global graph of all nodes.

info_allowExpand.description: Allow the user to pop-out the graph view to take up the whole screen

info_attractionForce.description: How much should linked nodes attract each other? This will make the graph appear more clustered.

info_linkLength.description: How long should the links between nodes be? The shorter the links the more connected nodes will cluster together.

info_repulsionForce.description: How much should nodes repel each other? This will make disconnected parts more spread out.

info_centralForce.description: How much should nodes be attracted to the center? This will make the graph appear more dense and circular.

info_edgePruning.description: Edges with a length above this threshold will not be rendered, however they will still contribute to the simulation. This can help large tangled graphs look more organised. Hovering over a node will still display these links.

info_minNodeRadius.description: How small should the smallest nodes be? The smaller a node is the less it will attract other nodes.

info_maxNodeRadius.description: How large should the largest nodes be? Nodes are sized by how many links they have. The larger a node is the more it will attract other nodes. This can be used to create a good grouping around the most important nodes.

info_allowResizing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be resized

info_allowCollapsing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be collapsed

info_rightDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the right sidebar

info_leftDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the left sidebar

info_selector.description: CSS selector for an element. The feature will be placed relative to this element.

info_type.description: Will this feature be placed before, after, or inside (at the beggining or end).

info_featurePlacement.description: Where to place this feature on the page. (Relative to the selector)

info_sourcePath.description: The local path to the source .html file which will be included.

info_allowFoldingLists.description: Whether or not to allow lists to be folded

info_allowFoldingHeadings.description: Whether or not to allow headings to be folded

info_siteUrl.description: The url that this site will be hosted at

info_authorName.description: The name of the author of the site
